title: Bugonia Deserved Better at the Oscars
text: Luke Foley's review celebrates director Yorgos Lanthimos's film Bugonia as a missed opportunity during awards season. The film follows two conspiracy theorists who kidnap a pharmaceutical CEO, believing she is an alien. Foley argues the movie represents a refined evolution of Lanthimos' singular style, combining his distinctive visual approach with mature storytelling. The narrative examines how societal alienation and corporate exploitation drive people toward extremism—symbolized through the motif of colony collapse disorder in bees. Jesse Plemons delivers what Foley considers an exceptional performance as Teddy, the rage-filled protagonist seeking answers for his mother's suffering at the hands of the pharmaceutical industry. Emma Stone plays the CEO with glib corporate talk and seething contempt, embodying the technocratic indifference Foley sees as central to contemporary American problems. The critic contends the Academy overlooked the film because it presented too bleak an assessment of America. While praising other Oscar winners like One Battle After Another, Foley suggests Bugonia offered something rarer: unflinching treatment of populist rage while steering clear of a neat solution.
